title: Texas Instruments Acquisition Resets Capex Path And Free Cash Flow Outlook
content:
Find your next quality investment with Simply Wall St's easy and powerful screener, trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Texas Instruments (NasdaqGS:TXN) has completed its US$7.5b acquisition of Silicon Labs, marking a major portfolio and scale change for the analog and embedded processing business. The company has also reduced its 2026 capital expenditure guidance, with management pointing to improved free cash flow and significant cost synergies tied to the integration. These moves represent a new phase for NasdaqGS:TXN, separate from recent product launch news, with implications for its cost base, product mix, and capital allocation priorities.

For you as an investor, this deal reshapes how NasdaqGS:TXN is positioned in semiconductors, particularly around analog and embedded solutions where long product cycles and broad end markets are common. The integration of Silicon Labs adds scale and a wider portfolio at a time when many chipmakers are reassessing capex, inventory, and customer exposure across industrial, automotive, and communications demand.

The lower 2026 capex guidance, together with management’s focus on free cash flow and cost synergies, puts capital efficiency and integration execution in the spotlight. Your attention now turns to how quickly NasdaqGS:TXN can capture those efficiencies, balance integration spending with its existing operations, and decide on priorities for any extra free cash flow, such as debt, dividends, or buybacks.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Texas Instruments by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Texas Instruments.NasdaqGS:TXN Earnings & Revenue Growth as at Apr 2026

We've flagged 3 risks for Texas Instruments. See which could impact your investment.

This acquisition and capex reset pulls Texas Instruments further toward a scale driven, analog and embedded processing model, while putting a stronger spotlight on integration and capital discipline. Folding Silicon Labs into the portfolio widens exposure to industrial, automotive and communications demand, which fits TI’s long product life, broad customer strategy and its narrative around resilient, higher margin end markets. At the same time, lower 2026 capex guidance and the expectation of higher free cash flow suggest management is now prioritizing returns on the large manufacturing build out it has already committed to. For you, the key questions are how efficiently TI can integrate a US$7.5b deal, whether the over US$450m of expected gross cost synergies actually show up in the cost base, and how any extra free cash flow is allocated between organic investment and shareholder returns. This move also sits alongside product activity like IsoShield based power modules, which could benefit from a broader analog and embedded catalog if TI can cross sell effectively against peers such as Analog Devices, Infineon and ON Semiconductor.

Story Continues

How This Fits Into The Texas Instruments Narrative

The Silicon Labs acquisition and capex pullback line up with the narrative that TI is leaning into long life, high margin industrial and automotive demand while seeking stronger free cash flow from its manufacturing footprint. Analysts had highlighted the risk of underutilized internal capacity, so a lower capex path could challenge earlier assumptions that TI would keep aggressively adding fabs regardless of demand visibility. The specific integration risks, cost synergy targets and any changes in product mix from Silicon Labs are not fully reflected in the broader narrative around automation, automotive content and U.S. manufacturing incentives.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Texas Instruments to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

⚠️ Large acquisitions can distract management and create execution risk if cultures, product roadmaps or sales channels between TI and Silicon Labs do not align as planned. ⚠️ If industrial, automotive or communications demand is weaker than expected, the combination of a bigger cost base and prior fab investments could pressure margins despite lower future capex. 🎁 The expected improvement in free cash flow, together with targeted cost synergies, could give TI more flexibility around dividends, buybacks and selective product investments. 🎁 A broader analog and embedded portfolio may help TI compete more effectively for system level sockets against Analog Devices, Infineon and ON Semiconductor, especially with customers seeking fewer, more resilient suppliers.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on TI’s integration milestones, including any disclosure on how quickly Silicon Labs is folded into manufacturing, R&D and sales, and whether management updates the US$450m synergy expectation. Listen for commentary on 2026 and beyond capex levels, free cash flow trends and how capital allocation priorities evolve, especially given TI’s history as a dividend payer. It is also worth following product roadmaps where Silicon Labs and TI technologies are combined, and whether TI gains or loses share in industrial and automotive sockets against key peers.

title: I'm Making a Bold Call on Texas Instruments Stock. Here's My Reasoning.
content:
Chipmaker Texas Instruments(NASDAQ: TXN) is largely being ignored by investors today because artificial intelligence (AI) chips are all the rage. Texas Instruments makes analog chips, which are older and simpler chips that convert real-world events into digital signals (think pressing a button). That's not sexy, but it is important. Which is why I'm making the bold call that boring Texas Instruments will eventually be a Dividend King.

Texas Instruments has a solid start

Texas Instruments is currently offering a very attractive 3% dividend yield. That's toward the high end of the stock's historical yield range. If you are a long-term dividend investor, like I am, now is probably a good time to be looking at Texas Instruments.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »Image source: Getty Images.

What's most exciting about Texas Instruments, however, is its 22-year-long streak of annual dividend increases. Right now, Texas Instruments is making capital investments in its business to prepare for future demand, and it has just announced its intention to buy Silicon Labs(NASDAQ: SLAB). Given that backdrop, the modest 4% dividend hike in 2025 is very reasonable and not the least bit worrying.

I predict there are more dividend hikes to come

My expectation is that Texas Instruments will eventually become a Dividend King. It will require 38 additional years of dividend growth to get there, but that isn't an outlandish expectation. The world is increasingly digital, and Texas Instruments makes chips that support the digital transition. They are simple chips, but they are also vital to the process.

Even AI can't exist without Texas Instruments' boring chips, noting that the company's sales to support data centers rose 70% year over year in the fourth quarter of 2025. While investors are focused on AI chipmakers like Nvidia(NASDAQ: NVDA), Texas Instruments continues to support the backbone that allows AI and a host of other technological advances to thrive. For example, your hot new cellphone probably has Texas Instruments chips, noting that the company has more than 100,000 customers and sells over 80,000 products.

More digital means more chips means more dividends

The logic behind my bold call that Texas Instruments will eventually become a Dividend King is very simple. The world needs what this industry-leading analog chip company makes if the digital transition is going to continue. If you love dividends as much as I do, you should check out Texas Instruments while its yield is still historically attractive.

Story Continues

Should you buy stock in Texas Instruments right now?

Before you buy stock in Texas Instruments, consider this:

The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Texas Instruments wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years.

Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $501,381!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,012,581!*

Now, it’s worth noting Stock Advisor’s total average return is 880% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 178% for the S&P 500. Don't miss the latest top 10 list, available with Stock Advisor, and join an investing community built by individual investors for individual investors.

*Stock Advisor returns as of April 1, 2026.

Reuben Gregg Brewer has positions in Texas Instruments. The Motley Fool has positions in and recommends Nvidia and Texas Instruments. The Motley Fool has a disclosure policy.

title: Is Ecolab (ECL) The Best Under-The-Radar AI Stock to Buy Now?
content:
We just covered Bill Gates’ 2026 Portfolio: Top 10 Stocks to Buy. Ecolab (NYSE:ECL) ranks #7 (see the Bill Gates’ 2026 Portfolio: Top 5 Stocks to Buy).

Water treatment company Ecolab (NYSE:ECL) has become a new AI play amid its exposure to liquid cooling solutions for data centers. How? The company recently announced to buy direct-to-chip liquid cooling CoolIT Systems for $4.75 billion. Ecolab (NYSE:ECL)’s closed-loop technology could solve the water-based cooling problem by replacing traditional, evaporative air cooling with sealed systems that recycle the same fluid indefinitely.

Ecolab (NYSE:ECL) is positioning itself as the “end-to-end” provider for the thousands of new data centers opening globally. The world faces a projected 56% water deficit by 2030. Ecolab’s solutions could become essential for tech and industrial businesses in the future. The stock ranks 7th in our list of the top stocks in Bill Gates’ 2026 portfolio.Silicon Laboratories (SLAB) Touches 4-Year High on $7.5-Billion Merger

While we acknowledge the potential of ECL as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. If you're looking for an extremely undervalued AI stock that also stands to benefit significantly from Trump-era tariffs and the onshoring trend, see our free report on the best short-term AI stock.

title: Silicon Laboratories (SLAB) and BANF Introduces In-Tire Sensor Platform
content:
Silicon Laboratories Inc. (NASDAQ:SLAB) is one of the High Growth Semiconductor Stocks to Buy. On March 10, Silicon Laboratories Inc. (NASDAQ:SLAB), along with BANF, which is a Korean specialist in intelligent tire systems, unveiled an in-tire sensor platform.

​Management noted that the platform is powered by the company’s ultra-low-power BG22 Bluetooth LE SoC. The platform has the ability to deliver real-time tire data for autonomous vehicles and connect fleets. This is significant as traditional tire pressure monitoring systems only alert on major pressure drops, missing early signs of fuel waste or safety issues. On the other hand, BANF’s iSensor flips this script, turning tires into “connected intelligence nodes” that process real-time data.

​In addition, the BG22 is mounted inside the tire and captures 3-axis acceleration, pressure, temperature, and tread depth at 4 kHz. The real-time data from the sensor is filtered into alerts for wheel-nut loosening, slip events, or low friction; this slashes data load and boosts response speed.

​Silicon Laboratories Inc. (NASDAQ:SLAB) provides and develops analog-intensive and mixed-signal integrated circuits. It specializes in wireless development platforms and product portfolios for the Internet of Things, including Bluetooth, sub-GHz proprietary technologies, Wi-SUN, Thread, Wi-Fi, Zigbee, and Z-Wave.

While we acknowledge the potential of SLAB as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. If you're looking for an extremely undervalued AI stock that also stands to benefit significantly from Trump-era tariffs and the onshoring trend, see our free report on the best short-term AI stock.
